mission.title: Install and Configure Bootc on Kubernetes
mission.description: Bootc is a tool designed for transactional, in-place operating system updates using OCI/Docker container images. Installing Bootc allows you to manage and upgrade your operating system seamlessly using container technology.

steps.title: Add the Bootc Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Bootc Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add bootc https://bootc-dev.github.io/bootc/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Bootc using Helm
steps.description:
Install Bootc in the 'bootc' namespace, creating it if it doesn't exist, and specify the version to install.
```bash
helm install bootc bootc/bootc --version v1.13.0 --namespace bootc --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Bootc pods are running successfully in the 'bootc' namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace bootc
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Update the Bootc deployment to set resource limits for better resource management.
```bash
kubectl set resources deployment bootc --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi --namespace bootc
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
Forward a local port to the Bootc service to access it from your local machine.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/bootc 8080:80 --namespace bootc
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Bootc will show the Bootc pods running in the 'bootc' namespace, and you will be able to access the service through port 8080 on your local machine. Resource limits will be applied to the Bootc deployment.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add bootc https://bootc-dev.github.io/bootc/charts
helm repo update
helm install bootc bootc/bootc --version v1.13.0 --namespace bootc --create-namespace
kubectl set resources deployment bootc --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi --namespace bootc

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description: Uninstall the Bootc release from the 'bootc' namespace using Helm.

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description: Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with Bootc.

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description: Delete the 'bootc' namespace and verify that all resources have been removed.

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description: Create a backup of the current Bootc release configuration and resources.

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description: Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Bootc release to the latest version.

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description: Check that the Bootc pods are running the new version successfully in the 'bootc' namespace.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
This issue occurs when the Bootc pods are unable to start successfully. Check the logs of the pods to diagnose the issue using the following command:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace bootc
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the Bootc service is not accessible, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly. Verify the service status with:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace bootc
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If the Bootc pods are not starting due to resource limits, check the resource allocation using:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment bootc --namespace bootc
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating that the Helm release is not found, verify that the release exists in the specified namespace:
```bash
helm list --namespace bootc
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and Kubectl installed on your local machine.
